result.documentation:
Split line into many lines, starting with the first matching bracket pair.

Note: this usually looks weird, only use this for function definitions.
Prefer RHS otherwise.  This is why this function is not symmetrical with
:func:`right_hand_split` which also handles optional parentheses.
result.name: left_hand_split

result.documentation:
Split line into many lines, starting with the last matching bracket pair.

If the split was by optional parentheses, attempt splitting without them, too.
`omit` is a collection of closing bracket IDs that shouldn't be considered for
this split.

Note: running this function modifies `bracket_depth` on the leaves of `line`.
result.name: right_hand_split

result.documentation:
Split the line into head, body, tail starting with the last bracket pair.

Note: this function should not have side effects. It's relied upon by
_maybe_split_omitting_optional_parens to get an opinion whether to prefer
splitting on the right side of an assignment statement.
result.name: _first_right_hand_split
_maybe_split_omitting_optional_parens
result.signature:
def _maybe_split_omitting_optional_parens(
    rhs: RHSResult,
    line: Line,
    mode: Mode,
    features: Collection[Feature] = (),
    omit: Collection[LeafID] = (),
) -> Iterator[Line]

result.documentation:
Returns whether we should prefer the result from a split omitting optional parens
(rhs_oop) over the original (rhs).
result.name: _prefer_split_rhs_oop_over_rhs

result.documentation:
Raise :exc:`CannotSplit` if the last left- or right-hand split failed.

Do nothing otherwise.

A left- or right-hand split is based on a pair of brackets. Content before
(and including) the opening bracket is left on one line, content inside the
brackets is put on a separate line, and finally content starting with and
following the closing bracket is put on a separate line.

Those are called `head`, `body`, and `tail`, respectively. If the split
produced the same line (all content in `head`) or ended up with an empty `body`
and the `tail` is just the closing bracket, then it's considered failed.
result.name: bracket_split_succeeded_or_raise
result.signature: def bracket_split_succeeded_or_raise(head: Line, body: Line, tail: Line) -> None

result.documentation:
Return a new line with given `leaves` and respective comments from `original`.

If it's the head component, brackets will be tracked so trailing commas are
respected.

If it's the body component, the result line is one-indented inside brackets and as
such has its first leaf's prefix normalized and a trailing comma added when
expected.
result.name: bracket_split_build_line
result.signature:
def bracket_split_build_line(
    leaves: List[Leaf],
    original: Line,
    opening_bracket: Leaf,
    *,
    component: _BracketSplitComponent,
) -> Line

result.documentation:
Normalize prefix of the first leaf in every line returned by `split_func`.

This is a decorator over relevant split functions.
result.name: dont_increase_indentation
result.signature: def dont_increase_indentation(split_func: Transformer) -> Transformer

result.name: _get_last_non_comment_leaf

result.name: _can_add_trailing_comma

result.name: _safe_add_trailing_comma
result.signature: def _safe_add_trailing_comma(safe: bool, delimiter_priority: int, line: Line) -> Line

result.documentation:
Split according to delimiters of the highest priority.

If the appropriate Features are given, the split will add trailing commas
also in function signatures and calls that contain `*` and `**`.
result.name: delimiter_split

result.documentation: Split standalone comments from the rest of the line.
result.name: standalone_comment_split

result.documentation:
Make existing optional parentheses invisible or create new ones.

`parens_after` is a set of string leaf values immediately after which parens
should be put.

Standardizes on visible parentheses for single-element tuples, and keeps
existing visible parentheses for other tuples and generator expressions.
result.name: normalize_invisible_parens

result.name: remove_await_parens

result.documentation:
When enabled and safe, wrap the multiple context managers in invisible parens.

It is only safe when `features` contain Feature.PARENTHESIZED_CONTEXT_MANAGERS.
result.name: _maybe_wrap_cms_in_parens

result.documentation: Recursively hide optional parens in `with` statements.

result.documentation:
If it's safe, make the parens in the atom `node` invisible, recursively.
Additionally, remove repeated, adjacent invisible parens from the atom `node`
as they are redundant.

Returns whether the node should itself be wrapped in invisible parentheses.
result.name: maybe_make_parens_invisible_in_atom

result.documentation: Should `line` be immediately split with `delimiter_split()` after RHS?

result.documentation:
Generate sets of closing bracket IDs that should be omitted in a RHS.

Brackets can be omitted if the entire trailer up to and including
a preceding closing bracket fits in one line.

Yielded sets are cumulative (contain results of previous yields, too).  First
set is empty, unless the line should explode, in which case bracket pairs until
the one that needs to explode are omitted.
result.name: generate_trailers_to_omit
result.signature: def generate_trailers_to_omit(line: Line, line_length: int) -> Iterator[Set[LeafID]]

result.name: get_grammars

result.documentation: Given a lib2to3 node, return its string representation.
result.name: lib2to3_unparse

result.name: _parse_single_version
parse_ast
result.signature: def parse_ast(src: str) -> ast.AST

result.name: _normalize
result.signature: def _normalize(lineend: str, value: str) -> str

result.documentation: Simple visitor generating strings to compare ASTs by content.
result.name: stringify_ast
result.signature: def stringify_ast(node: ast.AST, depth: int = 0) -> Iterator[str]

result.name: parse_line_ranges
result.signature: def parse_line_ranges(line_ranges: Sequence[str]) -> List[Tuple[int, int]]

result.documentation: Returns whether the line range is valid.
result.name: is_valid_line_range
result.signature: def is_valid_line_range(lines: Tuple[int, int]) -> bool

result.documentation:
Returns the adjusted line ranges based on edits from the original code.

This computes the new line ranges by diffing original_source and
modified_source, and adjust each range based on how the range overlaps with
the diffs.

Note the diff can contain lines outside of the original line ranges. This can
happen when the formatting has to be done in adjacent to maintain consistent
local results. For example:

1. def my_func(arg1, arg2,
2.             arg3,):
3.   pass

If it restricts to line 2-2, it can't simply reformat line 2, it also has
to reformat line 1:

1. def my_func(
2.     arg1,
3.     arg2,
4.     arg3,
5. ):
6.   pass

In this case, we will expand the line ranges to also include the whole diff
block.

Args:
  lines: a collection of line ranges.
  original_source: the original source.
  modified_source: the modified source.
result.name: adjusted_lines

result.documentation:
Converts unchanged lines to STANDALONE_COMMENT.

    The idea is similar to how `# fmt: on/off` is implemented. It also converts the
    nodes between those markers as a single `STANDALONE_COMMENT` leaf node with
    the unformatted code as its value. `STANDALONE_COMMENT` is a "fake" token
    that will be formatted as-is with its prefix normalized.

    Here we perform two passes:

    1. Visit the top-level statements, and convert them to a single
       `STANDALONE_COMMENT` when unchanged. This speeds up formatting when some
       of the top-level statements aren't changed.
    2. Convert unchanged "unwrapped lines" to `STANDALONE_COMMENT` nodes line by
       line. "unwrapped lines" are divided by the `NEWLINE` token. e.g. a
       multi-line statement is *one* "unwrapped line" that ends with `NEWLINE`,
       even though this statement itself can span multiple lines, and the
       tokenizer only sees the last '
' as the `NEWLINE` token.

    NOTE: During pass (2), comment prefixes and indentations are ALWAYS
    normalized even when the lines aren't changed. This is fixable by moving
    more formatting to pass (1). However, it's hard to get it correct when
    incorrect indentations are used. So we defer this to future optimizations.
result.name: convert_unchanged_lines
result.signature: def convert_unchanged_lines(src_node: Node, lines: Collection[Tuple[int, int]]) -> None

result.name: _contains_standalone_comment
result.signature: def _contains_standalone_comment(node: LN) -> bool

result.documentation:
A node visitor that converts unchanged top-level statements to
STANDALONE_COMMENT.

This is used in addition to _convert_unchanged_line_by_line, to
speed up formatting when there are unchanged top-level
classes/functions/statements.
result.name: _TopLevelStatementsVisitor
result.signature: class _TopLevelStatementsVisitor(Visitor[None])

result.documentation: Converts unchanged to STANDALONE_COMMENT line by line.
result.name: _convert_unchanged_line_by_line
result.signature: def _convert_unchanged_line_by_line(node: Node, lines_set: Set[int]) -> None

result.documentation: Convert node to STANDALONE_COMMENT by modifying the tree inline.
result.name: _convert_node_to_standalone_comment
result.signature: def _convert_node_to_standalone_comment(node: LN) -> None

result.documentation: Convert nodes to STANDALONE_COMMENT by modifying the tree inline.
result.name: _convert_nodes_to_standalone_comment
result.signature: def _convert_nodes_to_standalone_comment(nodes: Sequence[LN], *, newline: Leaf) -> None

result.documentation: Returns the line number of the leaf node's last line.
result.name: _leaf_line_end
result.signature: def _leaf_line_end(leaf: Leaf) -> int

result.documentation: Returns the line range of this node or list of nodes.
result.name: _get_line_range
result.signature: def _get_line_range(node_or_nodes: Union[LN, List[LN]]) -> Set[int]

result.documentation:
Returns a sequence of _LinesMapping by diffing the sources.

For example, given the following diff:
    import re
  - def func(arg1,
  -   arg2, arg3):
  + def func(arg1, arg2, arg3):
      pass
It returns the following mappings:
  original -> modified
   (1, 1)  ->  (1, 1), is_changed_block=False (the "import re" line)
   (2, 3)  ->  (2, 2), is_changed_block=True (the diff)
   (4, 4)  ->  (3, 3), is_changed_block=False (the "pass" line)

You can think of this visually as if it brings up a side-by-side diff, and tries
to map the line ranges from the left side to the right side:

  (1, 1)->(1, 1)    1. import re          1. import re
  (2, 3)->(2, 2)    2. def func(arg1,     2. def func(arg1, arg2, arg3):
                    3.   arg2, arg3):
  (4, 4)->(3, 3)    4.   pass             3.   pass

Args:
  original_source: the original source.
  modified_source: the modified source.
result.name: _calculate_lines_mappings

result.documentation: Returns the original index of the lines mappings for the original line.
result.name: _find_lines_mapping_index

result.documentation:
Reformat and print out `content` without spawning child processes.
Similar to `reformat_one`, but for string content.

`fast`, `write_back`, and `mode` options are passed to
:func:`format_file_in_place` or :func:`format_stdin_to_stdout`.
result.name: reformat_code
result.signature:
def reformat_code(
    content: str,
    fast: bool,
    write_back: WriteBack,
    mode: Mode,
    report: Report,
    *,

) -> None

`fast`, `write_back`, and `mode` options are passed to

result.documentation:
Format file under `src` path. Return True if changed.

If `write_back` is DIFF, write a diff to stdout. If it is YES, write reformatted
code to the file.
`mode` and `fast` options are passed to :func:`format_file_contents`.
result.name: format_file_in_place
result.signature:
def format_file_in_place(
    src: Path,
    fast: bool,
    mode: Mode,
    write_back: WriteBack = WriteBack.NO,
    lock: Any = None,  # multiprocessing.Manager().Lock() is some crazy proxy
    *,
    lines: Collection[Tuple[int, int]] = (),
) -> bool

result.documentation:
Format file on stdin. Return True if changed.

If content is None, it's read from sys.stdin.

If `write_back` is YES, write reformatted code back to stdout. If it is DIFF,
write a diff to stdout. The `mode` argument is passed to
:func:`format_file_contents`.
result.name: format_stdin_to_stdout
result.signature:
def format_stdin_to_stdout(
    fast: bool,
    *,
    content: Optional[str] = None,
    write_back: WriteBack = WriteBack.NO,
    mode: Mode,
    lines: Collection[Tuple[int, int]] = (),
) -> bool

Perform stability and equivalence checks.

Raise AssertionError if source and destination contents are not
equivalent, or if a second pass of the formatter would format the
content differently.
result.name: check_stability_and_equivalence

def check_stability_and_equivalence(

    dst_contents: str,
    *,
    mode: Mode,

) -> None

result.documentation:
Reformat contents of a file and return new contents.

If `fast` is False, additionally confirm that the reformatted code is
valid by calling :func:`assert_equivalent` and :func:`assert_stable` on it.
`mode` is passed to :func:`format_str`.
result.name: format_file_contents

def format_file_contents(
    src_contents: str,
    *,
    fast: bool,
    mode: Mode,
    lines: Collection[Tuple[int, int]] = (),
) -> FileContent

result.documentation:
Check that cell does not already contain TransformerManager transformations,
    or non-Python cell magics, which might cause tokenizer_rt to break because of
    indentations.

    If a cell contains ``!ls``, then it'll be transformed to
    ``get_ipython().system('ls')``. However, if the cell originally contained
    ``get_ipython().system('ls')``, then it would get transformed in the same way:

        >>> TransformerManager().transform_cell("get_ipython().system('ls')")
        "get_ipython().system('ls')
"
        >>> TransformerManager().transform_cell("!ls")
        "get_ipython().system('ls')
"

    Due to the impossibility of safely roundtripping in such situations, cells
    containing transformed magics will be ignored.
result.name: validate_cell
result.signature: def validate_cell(src: str, mode: Mode) -> None

result.name: format_cell
result.signature: def format_cell(src: str, *, fast: bool, mode: Mode) -> str

result.documentation:
If notebook is marked as non-Python, don't format it.

All notebook metadata fields are optional, see
https://nbformat.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format_description.html. So
if a notebook has empty metadata, we will try to parse it anyway.
result.name: validate_metadata
result.signature: def validate_metadata(nb: MutableMapping[str, Any]) -> None

result.documentation:
Format Jupyter notebook.

Operate cell-by-cell, only on code cells, only for Python notebooks.
If the ``.ipynb`` originally had a trailing newline, it'll be preserved.
result.name: format_ipynb_string
result.signature: def format_ipynb_string(src_contents: str, *, fast: bool, mode: Mode) -> FileContent

result.documentation:
Reformat a string and return new contents.

`mode` determines formatting options, such as how many characters per line are
allowed.  Example:

>>> import black
>>> print(black.format_str("def f(arg:str='')->None:...", mode=black.Mode()))
def f(arg: str = "") -> None:
    ...

A more complex example:

>>> print(
...   black.format_str(
...     "def f(arg:str='')->None: hey",
...     mode=black.Mode(
...       target_versions={black.TargetVersion.PY36},
...       line_length=10,
...       string_normalization=False,
...       is_pyi=False,
...     ),
...   ),
... )
def f(
    arg: str = '',
) -> None:
    hey
result.name: format_str
_format_str_once

result.documentation:
Return a tuple of (decoded_contents, encoding, newline).

`newline` is either CRLF or LF but `decoded_contents` is decoded with
universal newlines (i.e. only contains LF).
result.name: decode_bytes
result.signature: def decode_bytes(src: bytes) -> Tuple[FileContent, Encoding, NewLine]

parts.text:
The user is asking to retrieve a function based on its description. The description talks about transforming a line of code, evaluating it, and handling cases where the transformation is unsuccessful or needs a secondary attempt. The function also mentions input parameters like a line of code, a transformation function, a mode, features, and an optional string representation. The output is a list of transformed lines.

Looking at the `list_symbols` output, the function `right_hand_split` in `src/black/linegen.py` seems to fit the description. It takes a `line`, `mode`, and `features` as input, and it's related to transforming or splitting lines of code. The documentation also mentions attempting splits with and without optional parentheses, which aligns with the idea of secondary transformations or handling different conditions.

However, the user's description is quite generic and could potentially apply to other functions as well. To be precise, I need to find the *exact* function described. The description's detail about "applying a transformation... evaluating the transformed line... secondary transformation... additional constraints" strongly suggests a function that orchestrates transformations and validations.

Let's re-examine the `src/black/linegen.py` functions:
- `left_hand_split`: Splits from the left.
- `right_hand_split`: Splits from the right, with logic for optional parentheses.
- `_first_right_hand_split`: A helper for `right_hand_split`.
- `_maybe_split_omitting_optional_parens`: Handles splitting when optional parens are omitted.
- `_prefer_split_rhs_oop_over_rhs`: Decides preference between two split results.
- `bracket_split_succeeded_or_raise`: Validates if a bracket split was successful.
- `bracket_split_build_line`: Builds a line after a bracket split.
- `dont_increase_indentation`: A decorator, likely not the core function.
- `_get_last_non_comment_leaf`, `_can_add_trailing_comma`, `_safe_add_trailing_comma`: Helper functions.
- `delimiter_split`: Splits by delimiters.
- `standalone_comment_split`: Splits comments.
- `normalize_invisible_parens`: Normalizes parentheses.
- `_normalize_import_from`, `remove_await_parens`, `_maybe_wrap_cms_in_parens`, `remove_with_parens`, `maybe_make_parens_invisible_in_atom`: These seem to be focused on specific syntactic transformations.
- `should_split_line`, `generate_trailers_to_omit`: Helper functions.

The description's emphasis on "apply a transformation... evaluate... secondary transformation... additional constraints" and returning "a list of transformed lines" is still a bit ambiguous.

Let's consider the overall task again: "retrieve and repeat the exact described function". The description has 4 points: Purpose, Input, Output, Procedure. The procedure is the most detailed.

The description mentions "a transformation function" being passed as an argument. None of the `linegen.py` functions directly take a transformation function as a parameter in their signature as described.

However, the description *itself* reads like it's describing a function that *uses* other transformation functions. The description says "The function is designed to apply a transformation...".

Given the available functions, `right_hand_split` is the most plausible candidate because it's a primary line splitting/transformation function that takes `line`, `mode`, and `features` and returns an iterator of `Line` objects (which can be considered transformed lines). The mention of "secondary transformation" could relate to how `right_hand_split` tries splitting with and without optional parentheses.

Let me try to find a function that more explicitly matches the "transformation function" input. If none directly match, I'll stick with the best fit.

The description provided: "1. **Purpose**: The function is designed to apply a transformation to a line of code based on specified rules and conditions, and then evaluate the transformed line to ensure it meets certain criteria. If the initial transformation does not meet the criteria, a secondary transformation is attempted with additional constraints. 2. **Input**: The function accepts a line of code, a transformation function, a mode specifying formatting rules, a collection of features that may influence the transformation, and optionally a string representation of the line. 3. **Output**: It returns a list of transformed lines of code. If the transformation is unsuccessful or unnecessary, the original line or its direct transformation is returned. 4. **Procedure**: - Convert the input line to a string if not provided. - Apply the transformation function to the line. - Check if the transformed line is unchanged; if so, raise an exception. - Validate the transformed line against a set of criteria including line length and structural integrity. - If the initial transformation does not meet all criteria, clone the original line and attempt a secondary transformation with an additional feature. - Return the result of the transformation that best meets the criteria."

This description is very specific about taking a `transformation function` as an argument. None of the listed functions in `linegen.py` or other files take a `transformation function` as a direct argument in their signature.

Let's re-read the user's prompt: "Based on the function description and code context, retrieve and repeat the exact described function from the code context in a code block wrapped by triple backticks."

This implies the function *is* in the code context. It's possible the description is a bit of a simplified or generalized view of a more complex function's behavior, or that the "transformation function" is implicitly handled or composed.

Considering the functions again:
- `right_hand_split`: Takes `line`, `mode`, `features`. Returns `Iterator[Line]`. It does involve splitting (a form of transformation) and logic for optional parentheses (potential secondary attempts). The documentation says: "If the split was by optional parentheses, attempt splitting without them, too." This matches the "secondary transformation" idea.

The description also mentions "evaluating the transformed line to ensure it meets certain criteria" and "Validate the transformed line against a set of criteria including line length and structural integrity." The `bracket_split_succeeded_or_raise` function seems to do validation after a split.

However, the description is most strongly characterized by the input `transformation function`. If no function takes this explicitly, I cannot fulfill the request exactly.

Let's look at the `black/__init__.py` functions. `main` orchestrates everything. `reformat_code` and `reformat_one` are high-level. `format_file_in_place`, `format_stdin_to_stdout`, `format_file_contents`, `format_str` are the core formatting functions.

`format_str` takes `src_contents` and `mode`. It returns a formatted string. It doesn't take a "transformation function" as a parameter.

Perhaps the description is not of a single function but a *process* that some function implements. The description of `main` is very long and lists many parameters, but none is a "transformation function".

Given the constraint "retrieve and repeat the exact described function from the code context", and the fact that no function signature exactly matches the described inputs (specifically "transformation function"), I must conclude that the provided code context does not contain a function that *exactly* matches the description.

However, if I *must* pick the closest one, `right_hand_split` from `src/black/linegen.py` has characteristics that align with parts of the description (line transformation, secondary attempts via optional parens handling). But the "transformation function" argument is missing.

Let me assume the description is a high-level overview and try to find the *most* fitting function. The description's procedure details seem to describe a sophisticated line-splitting and formatting logic. `right_hand_split` is a key function in `linegen.py` which is about line generation.

The description states: "apply a transformation to a line of code based on specified rules and conditions". `right_hand_split` does this by splitting lines based on bracket pairs and mode.
"evaluate the transformed line to ensure it meets certain criteria". This could be implicitly done by the subsequent formatting steps or by helper functions like `bracket_split_succeeded_or_raise`.

result.documentation:
Split line into many lines, starting with the first matching bracket pair.

Note: this usually looks weird, only use this for function definitions.
Prefer RHS otherwise.  This is why this function is not symmetrical with
:func:`right_hand_split` which also handles optional parentheses.
result.name: left_hand_split

result.documentation:
Split line into many lines, starting with the last matching bracket pair.

If the split was by optional parentheses, attempt splitting without them, too.
`omit` is a collection of closing bracket IDs that shouldn't be considered for
this split.

Note: running this function modifies `bracket_depth` on the leaves of `line`.
result.name: right_hand_split

result.documentation:
Split the line into head, body, tail starting with the last bracket pair.

Note: this function should not have side effects. It's relied upon by
_maybe_split_omitting_optional_parens to get an opinion whether to prefer
splitting on the right side of an assignment statement.
result.name: _first_right_hand_split
_maybe_split_omitting_optional_parens
result.signature:
def _maybe_split_omitting_optional_parens(
    rhs: RHSResult,
    line: Line,
    mode: Mode,
    features: Collection[Feature] = (),
    omit: Collection[LeafID] = (),
) -> Iterator[Line]

result.documentation:
Returns whether we should prefer the result from a split omitting optional parens
(rhs_oop) over the original (rhs).
result.name: _prefer_split_rhs_oop_over_rhs

result.documentation:
Raise :exc:`CannotSplit` if the last left- or right-hand split failed.

Do nothing otherwise.

A left- or right-hand split is based on a pair of brackets. Content before
(and including) the opening bracket is left on one line, content inside the
brackets is put on a separate line, and finally content starting with and
following the closing bracket is put on a separate line.

Those are called `head`, `body`, and `tail`, respectively. If the split
produced the same line (all content in `head`) or ended up with an empty `body`
and the `tail` is just the closing bracket, then it's considered failed.
result.name: bracket_split_succeeded_or_raise
result.signature: def bracket_split_succeeded_or_raise(head: Line, body: Line, tail: Line) -> None

result.documentation:
Return a new line with given `leaves` and respective comments from `original`.

If it's the head component, brackets will be tracked so trailing commas are
respected.

If it's the body component, the result line is one-indented inside brackets and as
such has its first leaf's prefix normalized and a trailing comma added when
expected.
result.name: bracket_split_build_line
result.signature:
def bracket_split_build_line(
    leaves: List[Leaf],
    original: Line,
    opening_bracket: Leaf,
    *,
    component: _BracketSplitComponent,
) -> Line

Generate sets of closing bracket IDs that should be omitted in a RHS.

Brackets can be omitted if the entire trailer up to and including
a preceding closing bracket fits in one line.

Yielded sets are cumulative (contain results of previous yields, too).  First
set is empty, unless the line should explode, in which case bracket pairs until
the one that needs to explode are omitted.

result.documentation:
Returns the adjusted line ranges based on edits from the original code.

This computes the new line ranges by diffing original_source and
modified_source, and adjust each range based on how the range overlaps with
the diffs.

Note the diff can contain lines outside of the original line ranges. This can
happen when the formatting has to be done in adjacent to maintain consistent
local results. For example:

1. def my_func(arg1, arg2,
2.             arg3,):
3.   pass

If it restricts to line 2-2, it can't simply reformat line 2, it also has
to reformat line 1:

1. def my_func(
2.     arg1,
3.     arg2,
4.     arg3,
5. ):
6.   pass

In this case, we will expand the line ranges to also include the whole diff
block.

Args:
  lines: a collection of line ranges.
  original_source: the original source.
  modified_source: the modified source.
result.name: adjusted_lines

result.documentation:
Converts unchanged lines to STANDALONE_COMMENT.

    The idea is similar to how `# fmt: on/off` is implemented. It also converts the
    nodes between those markers as a single `STANDALONE_COMMENT` leaf node with
    the unformatted code as its value. `STANDALONE_COMMENT` is a "fake" token
    that will be formatted as-is with its prefix normalized.

    Here we perform two passes:

    1. Visit the top-level statements, and convert them to a single
       `STANDALONE_COMMENT` when unchanged. This speeds up formatting when some
       of the top-level statements aren't changed.
    2. Convert unchanged "unwrapped lines" to `STANDALONE_COMMENT` nodes line by
       line. "unwrapped lines" are divided by the `NEWLINE` token. e.g. a
       multi-line statement is *one* "unwrapped line" that ends with `NEWLINE`,
       even though this statement itself can span multiple lines, and the
       tokenizer only sees the last '
' as the `NEWLINE` token.

    NOTE: During pass (2), comment prefixes and indentations are ALWAYS
    normalized even when the lines aren't changed. This is fixable by moving
    more formatting to pass (1). However, it's hard to get it correct when
    incorrect indentations are used. So we defer this to future optimizations.
result.name: convert_unchanged_lines
result.signature: def convert_unchanged_lines(src_node: Node, lines: Collection[Tuple[int, int]]) -> None

result.name: _contains_standalone_comment
result.signature: def _contains_standalone_comment(node: LN) -> bool

result.documentation:
A node visitor that converts unchanged top-level statements to
STANDALONE_COMMENT.

This is used in addition to _convert_unchanged_line_by_line, to
speed up formatting when there are unchanged top-level
classes/functions/statements.
result.name: _TopLevelStatementsVisitor

result.documentation:
Returns a sequence of _LinesMapping by diffing the sources.

For example, given the following diff:
    import re
  - def func(arg1,
  -   arg2, arg3):
  + def func(arg1, arg2, arg3):
      pass
It returns the following mappings:
  original -> modified
   (1, 1)  ->  (1, 1), is_changed_block=False (the "import re" line)
   (2, 3)  ->  (2, 2), is_changed_block=True (the diff)
   (4, 4)  ->  (3, 3), is_changed_block=False (the "pass" line)

You can think of this visually as if it brings up a side-by-side diff, and tries
to map the line ranges from the left side to the right side:

  (1, 1)->(1, 1)    1. import re          1. import re
  (2, 3)->(2, 2)    2. def func(arg1,     2. def func(arg1, arg2, arg3):
                    3.   arg2, arg3):
  (4, 4)->(3, 3)    4.   pass             3.   pass

Args:
  original_source: the original source.
  modified_source: the modified source.
result.name: _calculate_lines_mappings

result.documentation: Returns the original index of the lines mappings for the original line.
result.name: _find_lines_mapping_index

result.documentation:
Check that cell does not already contain TransformerManager transformations,
    or non-Python cell magics, which might cause tokenizer_rt to break because of
    indentations.

    If a cell contains ``!ls``, then it'll be transformed to
    ``get_ipython().system('ls')``. However, if the cell originally contained
    ``get_ipython().system('ls')``, then it would get transformed in the same way:

        >>> TransformerManager().transform_cell("get_ipython().system('ls')")
        "get_ipython().system('ls')
"
        >>> TransformerManager().transform_cell("!ls")
        "get_ipython().system('ls')
"

    Due to the impossibility of safely roundtripping in such situations, cells
    containing transformed magics will be ignored.
result.name: validate_cell
result.signature: def validate_cell(src: str, mode: Mode) -> None

result.name: format_cell
result.signature: def format_cell(src: str, *, fast: bool, mode: Mode) -> str

result.documentation:
If notebook is marked as non-Python, don't format it.

All notebook metadata fields are optional, see
https://nbformat.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format_description.html. So
if a notebook has empty metadata, we will try to parse it anyway.
result.name: validate_metadata
result.signature: def validate_metadata(nb: MutableMapping[str, Any]) -> None

result.documentation:
Format Jupyter notebook.

Operate cell-by-cell, only on code cells, only for Python notebooks.
If the ``.ipynb`` originally had a trailing newline, it'll be preserved.
result.name: format_ipynb_string
result.signature: def format_ipynb_string(src_contents: str, *, fast: bool, mode: Mode) -> FileContent

result.documentation:
Reformat a string and return new contents.

`mode` determines formatting options, such as how many characters per line are
allowed.  Example:

>>> import black
>>> print(black.format_str("def f(arg:str='')->None:...", mode=black.Mode()))
def f(arg: str = "") -> None:
    ...

A more complex example:

>>> print(
...   black.format_str(
...     "def f(arg:str='')->None: hey",
...     mode=black.Mode(
...       target_versions={black.TargetVersion.PY36},
...       line_length=10,
...       string_normalization=False,
...       is_pyi=False,
...     ),
...   ),
... )
def f(
    arg: str = '',
) -> None:
    hey
result.name: format_str
_format_str_once

`newline` is either CRLF or LF but `decoded_contents` is decoded with
universal newlines (i.e. only contains LF).
result.name: decode_bytes
